channel.title: Master Feed (Updated every 30 mins)

item.title: Xi’s Flurry of Post-Trump Diplomacy
item.description: The Chinese president may be preparing for a rare and momentous trip to Pyongyang.

item.title: You probably don’t need extra electrolytes
item.description: Unless you’re athletic or unwell

item.title: Why Gender Inequality Still Haunts the Economy
item.description: Why do gender wage gaps and other disparities persist despite converging education levels and mounting evidence that the public opposes such forms of inequality? The problem lies less in outdated beliefs and biases than in entrenched structural constraints to women's economic advancement.

item.title: The Affordability Crisis Is About More than Prices
item.description: Too many governments around the world are responding to affordability concerns by treating the symptoms rather than the underlying causes. Negotiating marginal price reductions with retailers might create the appearance of responsiveness, but the results will inevitably disappoint.

item.title: China’s Fallen Generals Are Getting Unexpectedly Harsh Punishments
item.description: Chinese President Xi Jinping is raising the floor for punishing purge targets.

item.title: NATO Must Die
item.description: Some claim that the threats Europe is facing, especially after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, can create the momentum toward political union that the euro crisis and then the pandemic failed to generate. Right or wrong, one thing is clear: a functional defense union requires political union, and NATO's existence is inimical to it.

item.title: Is the United States on the Verge of Military Intervention in Cuba?
item.description: Seizing Castro could prove more costly and less effective than the capture of Maduro.

item.title: No Limits, No Good Options
item.description: Putin’s visit to China shows that Russia is getting sucked deeper into a profoundly unequal relationship.

item.title: A Turkish court ousts the opposition leader from his job
item.description: Deposing Ozgur Ozel is another step towards autocracy for Erdogan’s Turkey

item.title: Violence Looms Over Colombia’s Election
item.description: Three presidential candidates lead the field ahead of a first-round vote.

item.title: The Cultural Component of National Security
item.description: Since Russia’s full-scale invasion four years ago, Ukraine has become the world’s most consequential defense laboratory, trialing innovations that can strengthen European resilience. But the country has also demonstrated that national security depends as much on cultural identity as on military strength.

item.title: Geopolitical Chaos is Wrecking Pandemic Agreements
item.description: U.S. dysfunction is undercutting attempts at equality.

item.title: Geopolitical Chaos is Wrecking Pandemic Agreements
item.description: U.S. dysfunction is undercutting attempts at equality.

item.title: Keynes, Minsky, and the Economics of Uncertainty
item.description: Although Hyman Minsky’s version of John Maynard Keynes’s economics had a minimal impact on the mainstream during his own lifetime and in the years following, its central insights remain as relevant as ever. Both men understood that economic theory cannot get away with ignoring the fact of uncertainty.

item.title: US, Philippines on Cusp of Deal on Economic Security Zone, US Official Says
item.description: Undersecretary of State Jacob Helberg's comment came days after Philippine officials shot down a U.S. proposal that the 1,260-hectare zone be placed under U.S. jurisdiction.

item.title: How the West Misreads Modi’s Success
item.description: India may be less liberal now, but it remains democratic.

item.description: India may be less liberal now, but it remains democratic.

item.description: The Philippines has replaced India in Washington’s security calculus on China.

item.description: Internships are mandatory for the roughly 4 million children who graduate from secondary vocational schools each year. But some internships involve hazardous conditions – and even death.

item.description: The creation of five new districts has observers worried that New Delhi is trying to break Buddhist-Muslim solidarity in the restive region.

item.description: Turned off by U.S. policies and border practices, foreign visitors are going elsewhere.

item.title: Is Trump’s Iran War a Crime of Aggression?
item.description: America’s war on Iran is widely considered an illegal act of aggression in clear violation of the United Nations Charter. But assessing whether the war's planners should face prosecution raises difficult questions, largely owing to ongoing uncertainties about the meaning and scope of criminal aggression.

item.description: Leo Mirani explains how the column got its name

item.description: One summit was all symbolism; the other was all substance. China made it clear who its true friend is.

item.title: The Coming Vote for Europe’s Soul
item.description: The principle of non-discrimination between trading partners has underpinned international commerce for eight decades. Now, the same principle must guide European lawmakers as they decide whether to ratify the trade agreement reached last year between European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and US President Donald Trump.

item.title: South Korea’s Conservatives Are Running Out of Time
item.description: Adrift after Yoon’s impeachment, the PPP is facing the unthinkable: loss of support in its long-time base.

item.title: India’s Multialignment Test: Everything, Everywhere All at Once
item.description: The convergence of multiple theaters, crises, and strategic conversations at once makes this perhaps the most consequential test of India’s multialignment strategy.

item.title: Why Are China’s Young People Fed Up?
item.description: Decades of misguided economic policymaking have left China in a demographic hole that it seems incapable of climbing out of. As the “world’s factory,” the country is churning out everything except the people whom it will need to sustain its economic development and social stability over the long term.

item.description: India’s push for domestic defense production is running up against suppliers’ reluctance to transfer technology and key data.

item.title: A Cold Shower for the AI Mania
item.description: Although generative AI tools have improved rapidly and now outperform humans across many tasks, the market's current euphoria may not be justified. With AI firms increasingly resorting to debt financing, it is worth pausing to consider all the things that could go wrong.

item.title: Has Taiwan Made Itself Immune to American Betrayal?
item.description: Although Donald Trump's recent statements about Taiwan seemed like a gift to Chinese President Xi Jinping, such rhetoric no longer tells us as much as it once did. The durability of US-Taiwan relations now rests less on individual leaders than on institutional ties and economic integration, and both have been deepening.

item.title: The Duet of Command: Key Operational Issues for OPCON Transfer
item.description: The question is no longer whether to transfer OPCON, but how to design the alliance’s command structure and capabilities so that the post-transfer combined defense posture is demonstrably more efficient and more powerful.

item.title: India’s GDP Revisions Explained: What Changed and Why it Matters
item.description: India has revised its methodology for measuring economic growth after a decade. Better calculations matter, but the real test lies in how policymakers interpret them, and turn them into decisions that shape everyday life.

item.title: India Just Signed Its Most Consequential Chip Deal. The Hard Part Starts Now.
item.description: The Tata-ASML MoU puts India inside the world’s most exclusive technology partnership, but Beijing still controls the inputs used to make chips.

item.title: Australia’s Submarine Problems
item.description: In 2009, the Australian government recognized the need to replace its aging Collins-class submarines. What followed has been a mess of indecision, changing plans, and squandered opportunities.

item.title: Operationalizing Indo-Pacific Defense and Deterrence
item.description: Insights from Kimberly Lehn.

item.title: Why Trump Sued Himself
item.description: President Donald Trump has dropped his $10 billion suit against the Internal Revenue Service over the leak of his tax returns in exchange for a $1.776 billion “Anti-Weaponization Fund” to compensate Americans who claim persecution by the previous administration. The point is no longer to conceal corruption but to flaunt impunity.

item.title: How China Built a Closure That Cannot Leak
item.description: Four mechanical seals, an asymmetric one-way valve, and a 2,200-year social foundation make the 2026 closure airtight in a way no previous closure has been.

item.description: Safeguards exist on paper, but are development banks prepared to confront the political realities that prevent those safeguards from functioning in practice?

item.title: The US and China Must Learn to Coexist
item.description: The Beijing summit between Donald Trump and Xi Jinping showed that competition between the United States and China need not become a zero-sum struggle for supremacy. Even as the two countries vie for technological leadership, complete economic decoupling is neither feasible nor desirable.

item.title: The Price of Collusion: Why Sri Lanka Needs a New Approach to Debt Governance
item.description: By empowering local actors and fostering genuine competition, Sri Lanka can ensure that its debt serves the nation’s future, rather than the interests of a well-connected few.

item.title: The Gulf’s Post-OPEC Order
item.description: The United Arab Emirates’ decision to leave OPEC is the clearest expression yet of its desire to break from the Saudi-led regional order. The result could be a more fragmented Middle East marked by intensifying rivalries among Gulf powers, widening proxy conflicts, and chronic instability.

item.description: Nepal and Sri Lanka responded to comparable uprisings by redesigning the rules of the game. Bangladesh removed the opposing team from the field and called it a victory.

item.description: At least since the 1980s, American leaders visiting Beijing were traditionally expected to raise the Tibetan issue during bilateral meetings. Not anymore.

item.title: Sustainable Economies Will Own the Future
item.description: The COVID-19 pandemic and the wars in Ukraine and Iran have reframed the issue of sustainability, which is now as much about sovereignty and economic security as it is about planetary health. Countries and companies that fail to recognize this have everything to lose in the coming years.

item.title: The US Is Quietly Torpedoing Its Relationship With Pacific Island Partners
item.description: The Trump administration’s actions – or lack thereof – in its dealings with the FAS are undermining U.S. interests in the Pacific.

item.title: A Blueprint for Britain’s Revival
item.description: There are five big policy-related problems holding back the UK economy. Any politician who tackles them in earnest would benefit from a bond- and equity-market rally, a construction boom, higher business and consumer confidence, and the political dividends that come with broad-based gains to voters' standard of living.

item.description: China is translating the old concept of “encircling the cities from the countryside” for the digital age.

item.description: Kazakh oil and its connection to the Middle Corridor are placing the Turkiye-Kazakhstan relationship in a more critical position within Eurasian energy geopolitics.

item.description: Trump’s summit with Xi deferred the real Taiwan test: whether U.S. arms sales to Taipei will preserve credible support under strategic ambiguity, and how far Beijing will go to enforce its red line.

item.description: Trump was looking for a breakthrough, but not necessarily in the form of major trade deals. Trump’s mission was to insert charm and his own personality into the relationship with Xi.

item.description: People across Southeast Asian countries – Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore – want bank funding to end for all companies, including coal, that are making climate change worse.

item.title: Iran Is Pressing Its Strategic Advantage
item.description: Military and economic coercion usually costs the coercer as well as the target. But by threatening undersea cables beneath the Strait of Hormuz, Iran may have found a way to impose heavy costs on the US government and private sector while generating revenue for itself—at least in the short term.

item.title: Cartographic Justice for Africa
item.description: For centuries, the Mercator map has distorted Africa’s size, shaping perceptions of the continent's strategic significance and economic prospects. Adopting more accurate map projections is neither technically complex nor prohibitively expensive, yet the symbolic and material consequences could be profound.

item.title: The Indonesian Air Force’s Turkish Turn
item.description: Jakarta has recently signed onto several major Turkish aerospace industry programs with significant implications for the future of its airpower and domestic defense industry.

item.title: Indonesian Rupiah Falls to New Low as Global Oil Prices Surge
item.description: The slump was accompanied by a 4 percent drop in Jakarta's main stock index, which is under close scrutiny from the global index provider MSCI.

item.description: The dawn of a strange new European consensus.

item.description: Pope Leo takes the Vatican where Francis could not.

item.description: Pope Leo takes the Vatican where Francis could not.

item.description: The country occupies a uniquely strategic position due to its proven ability to balance competing geopolitical and economic forces.

item.title: Philippine Senate Kicks Off Impeachment Trial of Vice President Sara Duterte
item.description: Whatever the trial's outcome, the political war between Duterte and President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. is unlikely to come to a conclusive end.

item.title: With an ‘Obstacle’ Gone, Will the Indian Government Finally Seal the Teesta Deal With Bangladesh?
item.description: An agreement to share the waters of the Teesta River will not go down well in northern West Bengal.

item.title: Canada’s Great Recalibration
item.description: The growing fusion of economics, security, and technology has altered the nature of 21st-century interdependence, and Canada has responded by being more intentional. Rather than automatically serving as an extension of US-centered systems, it is creating more options across a wider network of relationships.

item.title: From Jefferson to Nehru
item.description: The Declaration of Independence continues to challenge countries, including the US and India, to measure themselves against the ideals they profess. But in a century marked by rising inequality, an authoritarian resurgence, and the emergence of new forms of domination, measuring up will become only more difficult.

item.title: Jefferson’s Revolutionary Language
item.description: Of the delegates who assembled in Philadelphia in 1776 for the Second Continental Congress, only Thomas Jefferson possessed the skill, knowledge, and linguistic gifts to make the Declaration of Independence an immortal text. He was the self-appointed conscience of America, and sensational imagery was his stock-in-trade.

item.title: Free-Market Capitalism Is Destroying US Democracy
item.description: Far from two sides of the same coin, free-market capitalism and democracy are fundamentally incompatible. The only way to restore US democracy is to transform the system, so that it compensates innovators but limits their market power, while ensuring that the benefits of economic progress are more equitably shared.

item.title: The Roots of Democratic Decline
item.description: Notwithstanding recent setbacks for illiberal, populist, and authoritarian leaders in some countries, good-governance groups and experts on democratic decline continue to document a disturbing global trend toward “autocratization.” What remains to be seen is whether enough voters will wake up to the threat.

item.title: China’s AI Agenda
item.description: While American and international news coverage of AI tends to focus on a handful of Silicon Valley labs, China's more pragmatic approach to leveraging the technology could ultimately be the bigger story. What the Chinese leadership sees in the technology is nothing less than a master key for its economic ambitions.

item.title: After the Liberal Order
